content:
Hi everyone! :wave:

I'm a Master's student, and I've attended for personal interest a course on Linux kernel hacking; as a final assignment, I'd like to make my first contribution to the Linux kernel via the Rust for Linux project.

I've had a look at the [good first issue] tag of the GitHub repository, but it seems that all of the issues there are already being worked on by someone (except for #1063, which in my eyes seems to be too trivial for a final assignment, and so I fear the professor might not accept it).

Is there any other task you'd like or recommend me to pick up and work on myself?
I keep a loose project idea board, anything there look interesting to you? https://github.com/tgross35/RFL-patch-registry/?tab=readme-ov-file#project-idea-board
Or probably a better question, what are you interested in?

• iio driver: sounds interesting and not too complex, but I'm not familiar at all with industrial sensors; would I need to have one to test the driver? Or are there specifications I can follow and potentially mock?

• phy driver: by skimming a datasheet of one of the proposed chips it feels like it would be a lot more work than how much I'm expected to do; if it was just for me I'd be OK with it, but sadly I need to graduate sometime, so I cannot yet accept something of that size :sweat_smile:

• tc actions: that sounds even more interesting than the previous two, and much more documented and accessible to me, therefore it should be easier for me to work on it; has there been any work on it since the email thread in January linked in the idea board? Should I join someone in the development of an action, or start from scratch and implement something like the act_simple.c action linked in the email thread?

Thanks for the reply by the way!
It's been very helpful already :D
There aren't any new updates on these to my knowledge

I don't think there is anything really "industrial" about industrial IO; this could just be a new temp sensor or ADC that plugs into a raspberry pi, or something you already have on your montherboard. Having the physical hardware to test is always preferable though not strictly required (you can send your patches with a request for testing, but it's not like you want to develop vicariously through somebody else who has the hardware).

As for what I'd be personally interested in... any subsystem is fine, but I have a constraint on the estimate amount of work I'd need to put on of 1-3 weeks for it to be accepted as assignment.

After finding an available task, I'll be submitting it to my professor (the maintainer of bfq) for confirmation that it meets the requirements he set out.
Oh, hi!

this could just be a new temp sensor or ADC that plugs into a raspberry pi

Oh, that could very well work too then!

Usually when somebody works on a new subsystem / driver type, they first mimic an existing C driver (so you have something to test against and a proof of concept), and then write one in Rust for something new that will actually get upstreamed. Duplicates of existing C drivers don't get upstreamed (very few exceptions exist), nor do bindings that don't have an in-kernel driver using them.
Sounds like writing a duplicate of the act_simple action could work really well then!
As a proof of concept that would be great, just know it wouldn't get merged
Yeah, that would do perfectly for now!
Sending an email right now to my professor to ask if it's an acceptable task; many thanks again for the feedback!

Steffo said:

estimate amount of work I'd need to put on of 1-3 weeks for it to be accepted as assignment

What exactly needs to be accomplished in 1-3 weeks? You could probably write a clone of act_simple (though even that may be cutting it close if you're new to the kernel), but that certainly isn't enough time for something to go through the review process
Which I suppose is okay for you if you're not planning to upstream anything
Oh, it's fine if the review process takes more, it's more about the "work hours" I need to put in, since I can study something else while waiting for feedback from the kernel mailing lists...
Double check that there isn't a PR open or a patch sent with act_simple.rs, I think somebody might have been looking at one (@Valentin Obst?)
Oh, I'll do immediately

Can you clarify that it is okay that your work does not get merged upstream? You would need to write a new action after making a clone of act_simple.

You can still send an RFC patchset for informal review and to kick off others' work (I'd be happy to review if I am available), but obviously we don't want you to do poorly on your assignment if actually getting picked up is a requirement.

Yeah, it's perfectly fine if the work doesn't get merged upstream, other students who got a very good grade have been working on similar patches which didn't get merged and the professor was fine with it.

I'll double check with him in case, thanks for the concern :)
Email sent; marking this as resolved for now, I'll reopen the topic if something else is needed, otherwise I'll make a different one for the specific work I'll be performing. Thanks!
Oh, wait, I don't have permission to do so, nevermind...
Trevor Gross has marked this topic as resolved.

Trevor Gross said:

Double check that there isn't a PR open or a patch sent with act_simple.rs, I think somebody might have been looking at one (Valentin Obst?)

I did some experimenting but got side tracked. Didn't push it anywhere so there isn't anything public from my side.

Thanks for been interesting in working on this! Happy to review it as well when you need feedback.

Sorry to say that my professor rejected my proposal, and would rather have me work on something else...
Maybe I'll drop by again in the future when I'm done with university!
Thanks for all the help :)
That's too bad! Hope you get a good project, feel free to drop by any time
